description: This figure presents the iron-rich corner of the Fe–Mn–Al ternary phase diagram for alloys containing approximately 1 wt% C at 1150 °C, illustrating the compositional boundaries between single-phase and two-phase fields. The diagram employs a triangular coordinate grid with Fe at the left vertex, Mn (0–50%) along the horizontal axis, and Al (0–30%) on the vertical axis. Two experimentally determined boundaries are shown: curve 1 (solid circles) marks alloys exhibiting a single-phase austenitic (γ-solid solution) structure, while curve 2 (open circles) delineates alloys with a two-phase structure of γ- and α-solid solutions. A bold line connects the data points to indicate the phase boundary between these regions. The figure demonstrates that higher Mn contents at a given Al level favor the retention of a single-phase γ-field, whereas lower Mn or more Fe-rich compositions fall within the γ + α two-phase region. This diagram provides the compositional basis for selecting alloys (e.g., in the range of ~25–30% Mn and ~8–10% Al) that are fully austenitic upon quenching from 1150 °C, which underpins the subsequent heat-treatment and property studies.

Figure 2 presents four optical micrographs comparing the microstructures of two Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys: alloy I (25% Mn, 10% Al, 1% C) and alloy II (20% Mn, 10% Al, 1% C). The top-left micrograph shows alloy I after quenching from 1150°C, while the top-right image (referenced as Fig.2b) depicts the same alloy after quenching plus aging at 600°C for 50 h. The bottom-left image, marked II a, shows alloy II in the as-quenched condition at ×150, and the bottom-right image, marked b, shows alloy II after identical aging at ×500. According to the caption and text, the quenched structures are austenitic, whereas aging induces pronounced phase decomposition: the 20% Mn alloy develops austenite and ferrite with carbides at austenite grain boundaries, occasionally surrounded by acicular austenite (Fig.2,b, II), while the 25% Mn alloy exhibits austenite with a dispersed phase precipitated along grain boundaries, producing higher etchability. These transformations accompany a sharp hardness increase from HRB 86–88 to HRC 35–37. The figure thus evidences that manganese content governs γ-phase stability, the extent of retained austenite, and the morphology of grain-boundary precipitates during aging.

This figure presents the mechanical properties of an Fe-Mn-Al-C alloy (27.5% Mn, 9.1% Al, 0.92% C) as a function of aging temperature after a 16-hour heat treatment, with the x-axis showing temperature in °C from approximately 450 to 575°C. Four curves are plotted: σb (ultimate tensile strength, solid line with circles, in kg/mm²), σ0.2 (yield strength, dashed line with circles, in kg/mm²), ψ (reduction of area, solid line with circles, in %), and δ (elongation, solid line with circles, in %). The σb curve rises with temperature, peaks near 550°C at approximately 95 kg/mm², and then declines, while σ0.2 increases steadily up to 550°C reaching roughly 80 kg/mm². In contrast, both ductility indicators ψ and δ decrease sharply with increasing aging temperature, reaching minima near 550°C (ψ ≈ 10% and δ ≈ 5–8%) before recovering slightly at higher temperatures. The figure supports the core conclusion that peak strength in this ~9% Al alloy occurs around 550°C at the expense of severe ductility loss, and that above 550°C strength drops while ductility recovers due to coalescence of the dispersed phase.
